1:
"Now Moses kept the flock of Jethro his father in law, the priest of Midian: and he led the flock to the backside of the desert, and came to the mountain of God, even to Horeb." The witnesses note Moses keeping Jethro's flock, leading it to Horeb, the mountain of God. Gill notes Moses kept the flock of Jethro his father-in-law, the priest of Midian, who was either Reuel or a son of his; he led the flock to the backside of the desert and came to Horeb. The witnesses note Moses keeping the flock of Jethro and leading it to Horeb, the mountain of God; the lowly shepherd-life of Moses, the setting of the great call — that Moses, once a prince, now a humble shepherd, is found at his daily work: "Moses kept the flock of Jethro his father in law... and came to the mountain of God, even to Horeb"; the witnesses note the lowliness of his estate, that Moses, who had been raised in Pharaoh's court, now "kept the flock" of his father-in-law, a plain shepherd in the wilderness, forty years in this hidden, humble life; the witnesses note the leading of the flock "to the backside of the desert," far into the wilderness, and his coming "to the mountain of God, even to Horeb" (called so by anticipation, for there God would after give the law); the witnesses note the providence, that Moses, all unknowing, was led to the very place of his calling, God meeting him in the path of his common duty; the witnesses note that God often calls his servants while they are faithfully busy at their lowly work; so that Moses the shepherd comes to the mountain of God; and came to the mountain of God, even to Horeb. The witnesses note Now Moses kept the flock of Jethro his father in law, the priest of Midian. The witnesses note and came to the mountain of God, even to Horeb.

The witnesses note Moses the lowly shepherd, once a prince of Egypt, led with Jethro's flock to Horeb, the mountain of God — God meeting him in the path of his common duty; often God calls his servants while they are faithfully busy at their humble work.

So the witnesses make the verse the lowly shepherd-life that sets the great call. The lesson the expositors draw is that Moses, now a humble shepherd, was led to Horeb in the path of his daily duty, God meeting him at his common work. The witnesses note the kept the flock: "Moses kept the flock of Jethro" — Gill notes his father-in-law; the kept the flock; the his father-in-law; the the priest of Midian; the a shepherd; the lowly. The expositors observe the once a prince: once raised in Pharaoh's court; the once a prince; the a prince; the the court; the now a shepherd; the humble. The witnesses press the backside of the desert: "the backside of the desert" — far into the wilderness; the backside of the desert; the the desert; the far; the the wilderness; the led. The expositors draw the lesson on the mountain of God: "the mountain of God, even to Horeb" — the place of the law; the mountain of God; the Horeb; the the law; the after; the by anticipation. The witnesses observe the led unknowing: led all unknowing to his calling; the led unknowing; the his calling. The expositors note the at common duty: God meets him at common duty; the at common duty; the his work; the faithful. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and came to the mountain of God even to Horeb, and the shepherd-life. The verse thus records the lowly shepherd-life — teaching that Moses was led to Horeb in the path of his daily duty, God meeting him at his common work.
2:
"And the angel of the LORD appeared unto him in a flame of fire out of the midst of a bush: and he looked, and, behold, the bush burned with fire, and the bush was not consumed." The witnesses note the Angel of the LORD appearing in the flaming bush that burned unconsumed. Gill notes the Angel of the LORD was not a created angel, but the Angel of God's presence and covenant, the eternal Word and Son of God, since he is afterwards expressly called Jehovah. The witnesses note the Angel of the LORD appearing to Moses in a flame of fire out of the bush, which burned yet was not consumed; the wondrous theophany, the divine Angel in the unconsumed bush — that God appears in fire to call his servant: "the angel of the LORD appeared unto him in a flame of fire out of the midst of a bush... and the bush was not consumed"; the witnesses note who the "Angel of the LORD" was, not a created angel but, as Gill marks, "the Angel of God's presence and covenant, the eternal Word and Son of God," for he is afterward called "Jehovah" and "God" (vv.4, 6), a pre-incarnate appearing of Christ; the witnesses note the manner, "in a flame of fire out of the midst of a bush" — God appearing in fire, the emblem of his holiness and majesty; the witnesses note the great wonder, "the bush burned with fire, and the bush was not consumed" — that the lowly thorn-bush blazed yet was not burnt up; the witnesses note the rich figure the commentators draw, of Israel (and the church) in the fire of affliction in Egypt, burning yet not consumed, because God was in the midst; the witnesses note that what cannot be consumed when God is present is a comfort to his suffering people; so that the Angel of the LORD appears in the unconsumed bush; and the bush was not consumed. The witnesses note the angel of the LORD appeared unto him in a flame of fire out of the midst of a bush. The witnesses note and, behold, the bush burned with fire, and the bush was not consumed.

The witnesses note the Angel of the LORD — not a created angel but the eternal Word and Son of God, since he is afterward called Jehovah — appearing in the flaming bush that burned yet was not consumed; a figure of Israel in the fire of affliction, not consumed because God was in the midst.

So the witnesses make the verse the theophany of the unconsumed bush. The lesson the expositors draw is that the Angel of the LORD, the eternal Son of God, appeared in the flaming bush, a figure of Israel in affliction, burning yet not consumed because God was present. The witnesses note the Angel of the LORD: "the angel of the LORD appeared" — Gill notes the eternal Word and Son of God; the Angel of the LORD; the the Son of God; the called Jehovah. The witnesses press the flame of fire: "in a flame of fire" — the emblem of holiness; the flame of fire; the holiness; the majesty. The expositors draw the lesson on the not consumed: "the bush was not consumed" — burned yet not burnt; the not consumed; the the thorn-bush; the the wonder. The expositors note the God in the midst: not consumed, God in the midst; the God in the midst; the present; the the comfort. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and behold the bush burned with fire and the bush was not consumed, and the unconsumed bush. The verse thus records the theophany of the unconsumed bush — teaching that the Angel of the LORD appeared in the flaming bush, a figure of Israel not consumed because God was present.
3:
"And Moses said, I will now turn aside, and see this great sight, why the bush is not burnt." The witnesses note Moses resolving to turn aside and see the great sight. Gill notes Moses said he would turn aside from the place where he was and the flock he was feeding, and get nearer to the bush, which was on one side of him. The witnesses note Moses saying he would turn aside to see the great sight, why the bush was not burnt; the kindling of Moses' wonder and his drawing near to the marvel — that the strange sight draws Moses to look closer: "I will now turn aside, and see this great sight, why the bush is not burnt"; the witnesses note the wonder awakened, that Moses, seeing the bush blaze yet not burn, was moved with holy curiosity to "turn aside" from his flock and "see this great sight"; the witnesses note the question that drew him, "why the bush is not burnt" — that the marvel was not the fire only but that the fire did not consume, a thing against nature, which made Moses seek to know its cause; the witnesses note that God uses the wonder to draw Moses near, that he might there meet and call him; the witnesses note the readiness of Moses to leave his task and inquire into the things of God when God's hand was so plainly in it; so that Moses turns aside to behold the great sight; why the bush is not burnt. The witnesses note And Moses said, I will now turn aside, and see this great sight. The witnesses note why the bush is not burnt.

The witnesses note Moses moved with holy wonder to turn aside and see why the bush was not burnt — the marvel not the fire only but that it did not consume; God using the wonder to draw Moses near, that he might there meet and call him.

So the witnesses make the verse Moses drawn by wonder to the marvel. The lesson the expositors draw is that Moses turned aside to see the great sight, the bush burning yet not consumed, God using the wonder to draw him near to his calling. The witnesses note the I will turn aside: "I will now turn aside" — Gill notes from his flock; the I will turn aside; the from his flock; the get nearer; the to the bush; the the marvel. The expositors observe the great sight: "see this great sight" — the wonder awakened; the great sight; the the wonder; the holy curiosity; the to see; the moved. The witnesses press the not burnt: "why the bush is not burnt" — against nature; the not burnt; the against nature; the the fire; the not consume; the the cause. The expositors draw the lesson on the the marvel: the marvel that drew him; the the marvel; the the marvel; the drew him; the closer; the to look. The witnesses observe the God draws near: God draws Moses near; the God draws near; the God; the draws near; the to meet; the to call. The expositors note the leaves his task: ready to leave his task and inquire; the leaves his task; the leaves; the his task; the inquire; the God's hand. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And Moses said I will now turn aside and see this great sight, the why the bush is not burnt, and the wonder. The verse thus records Moses drawn by wonder — teaching that Moses turned aside to see the great sight, God using the wonder to draw him near to his calling.
4:
"And when the LORD saw that he turned aside to see, God called unto him out of the midst of the bush, and said, Moses, Moses. And he said, Here am I." The witnesses note God calling Moses by name out of the bush, and Moses answering. Gill notes he who is before called the Angel of the LORD is here Jehovah, the omniscient and omnipresent Being, who observed Moses turning aside and called to him. The witnesses note the LORD, seeing Moses turn aside, calling to him out of the bush, "Moses, Moses," and Moses answering, "Here am I"; the personal call of God by name, and Moses' ready answer — that God, who is the Angel and now named Jehovah, calls his servant: "God called unto him out of the midst of the bush, and said, Moses, Moses. And he said, Here am I"; the witnesses note the identity confirmed, that "the LORD" and "God" here are the same as "the Angel of the LORD" (v.2), so that the Angel of the bush is Jehovah, the omniscient and omnipresent God; the witnesses note the personal and earnest call, "Moses, Moses" — the doubling of the name marking love, earnestness, and the weight of the call, as God elsewhere calls "Abraham, Abraham" and "Samuel, Samuel"; the witnesses note Moses' ready and reverent answer, "Here am I" — the response of a willing servant, present and attentive to God's voice; the witnesses note that God knows and calls his servants by name, and looks for their ready answer; so that God calls Moses by name, and Moses answers; And he said, Here am I. The witnesses note God called unto him out of the midst of the bush, and said, Moses, Moses. The witnesses note And he said, Here am I.

The witnesses note God — the Angel now named Jehovah — calling "Moses, Moses" out of the bush, the doubled name marking love and earnestness; and Moses' ready, reverent "Here am I," the answer of a willing servant present to God's voice.

So the witnesses make the verse God's personal call and Moses' ready answer. The lesson the expositors draw is that God called Moses by name from the bush, the Angel here named Jehovah, and Moses answered as a willing servant. The witnesses note the the LORD saw: "when the LORD saw that he turned aside" — Gill notes the omniscient God; the the LORD saw; the the omniscient; the omnipresent; the observed; the Moses. The expositors observe the the Angel is Jehovah: the Angel now named Jehovah; the the Angel is Jehovah; the the Angel; the Jehovah; the the same; the God. The witnesses press the Moses, Moses: "Moses, Moses" — the doubled name; the Moses, Moses; the the doubled name; the love; the earnestness; the the weight. The expositors draw the lesson on the as Abraham, Samuel: as God called Abraham and Samuel; the as Abraham, Samuel; the Abraham; the Samuel; the doubled; the called. The witnesses observe the Here am I: "Here am I" — the willing servant; the Here am I; the willing; the servant; the present; the attentive. The expositors note the calls by name: God calls his servants by name; the calls by name; the by name; the his servants; the knows; the ready answer. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the God called unto him out of the midst of the bush and said Moses Moses, the And he said Here am I, and the personal call. The verse thus records God's personal call and Moses' answer — teaching that God called Moses by name from the bush, the Angel here named Jehovah, and Moses answered as a willing servant.
5:
"And he said, Draw not nigh hither: put off thy shoes from off thy feet, for the place whereon thou standest is holy ground." The witnesses note God bidding Moses keep his distance and put off his shoes, the ground being holy. Adam Clarke notes it is likely that from this circumstance all the eastern nations have agreed to perform the acts of religious worship barefooted. The witnesses note God bidding Moses not draw near but put off his shoes, for the place was holy ground; the demand of reverence in the presence of the holy God — that God teaches Moses awe before he teaches him his errand: "Draw not nigh hither: put off thy shoes from off thy feet, for the place whereon thou standest is holy ground"; the witnesses note the charge to keep distance, "Draw not nigh hither" — that Moses, a sinful man, was not to rush rashly into the immediate presence of the holy God, but to keep a reverent distance; the witnesses note the token of reverence required, "put off thy shoes from off thy feet" — the Eastern sign of respect and submission, putting off the shoes as a lowly servant on entering a holy or honoured place, a custom Clarke notes spread among the eastern nations for worship; the witnesses note the reason, "for the place whereon thou standest is holy ground" — not that the ground was holy in itself, but made holy by the presence of God then manifested there; the witnesses note the great lesson, that God is to be approached with reverence and godly fear, for he is holy; so that Moses is taught reverence before the holy God; for the place whereon thou standest is holy ground. The witnesses note Draw not nigh hither: put off thy shoes from off thy feet. The witnesses note for the place whereon thou standest is holy ground.

The witnesses note God bidding Moses keep his distance and put off his shoes, for the ground was made holy by God's presence — teaching awe before the errand; God is to be approached with reverence and godly fear, for he is holy.

So the witnesses make the verse the demand of reverence before the holy God. The lesson the expositors draw is that Moses was to keep distance and put off his shoes, the ground made holy by God's presence, that God may be approached with reverence and godly fear. The witnesses note the draw not nigh: "Draw not nigh hither" — keep a reverent distance; the draw not nigh; the keep distance; the reverent; the not rashly; the a sinful man. The expositors observe the put off thy shoes: "put off thy shoes" — Clarke notes the Eastern sign; the put off thy shoes; the Clarke; the the Eastern sign; the respect; the submission. The witnesses press the the custom: a custom spread for worship; the the custom; the spread; the eastern nations; the worship; the barefoot. The expositors draw the lesson on the holy ground: "holy ground" — made holy by God's presence; the holy ground; the holy; the God's presence; the not in itself; the manifested. The witnesses observe the reverence required: reverence in God's presence; the reverence required; the reverence; the God's presence; the the holy God; the awe. The expositors note the godly fear: approached with godly fear; the godly fear; the godly fear; the the lesson; the he is holy; the approach. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Draw not nigh hither put off thy shoes from off thy feet, the for the place whereon thou standest is holy ground, and the reverence. The verse thus records the demand of reverence — teaching that the ground was made holy by God's presence, that God may be approached with reverence and godly fear.
6:
"Moreover he said, I am the God of thy father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob. And Moses hid his face; for he was afraid to look upon God." The witnesses note God declaring himself the God of the fathers, and Moses hiding his face. Adam Clarke notes that though the word "father" is here used in the singular, it points to the patriarchs collectively, the God of his fathers. The witnesses note God saying, "I am the God of thy father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob," and Moses hiding his face in fear; the covenant title of God and the awe of Moses before him — that God names himself the covenant God of the patriarchs: "I am the God of thy father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob"; the witnesses note the great covenant name, "the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob" — that God makes himself known not by his bare being only but as the God who had covenanted with the fathers, faithful to his promises, now coming to fulfil them in delivering their seed; the witnesses note the weight our Lord drew from these words, that "God is not the God of the dead, but of the living," proving the resurrection and the life of the patriarchs with God; the witnesses note the effect on Moses, "Moses hid his face; for he was afraid to look upon God" — the awe of a sinful creature before the holy and glorious God, not daring to gaze on his presence; the witnesses note the godly fear that the manifest presence of God works in his servants; so that God names himself the covenant God, and Moses hides his face; for he was afraid to look upon God. The witnesses note I am the God of thy father, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob. The witnesses note And Moses hid his face; for he was afraid to look upon God.

The witnesses note God naming himself the covenant God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob — faithful to his promises, now coming to fulfil them; the words from which Christ proved the resurrection ("not the God of the dead, but of the living"); and Moses hiding his face in holy fear.

So the witnesses make the verse the covenant title of God and the awe of Moses. The lesson the expositors draw is that God made himself known as the covenant God of the fathers, faithful to fulfil his promises, and Moses hid his face in holy fear. The witnesses note the God of thy father: "I am the God of thy father" — Clarke notes the patriarchs collectively; the God of thy father; the the fathers. The witnesses press the to fulfil his promises: now coming to fulfil his promises; the to fulfil his promises; the their seed; the deliver. The expositors draw the lesson on the the resurrection: Christ proved the resurrection from this; the the resurrection; the not of the dead; the the living. The witnesses observe the hid his face: "Moses hid his face" — holy fear; the hid his face; the afraid; the the awe. The expositors note the godly fear: the godly fear God's presence works; the godly fear; the his servants; the awe. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And Moses hid his face for he was afraid to look upon God, and the covenant title. The verse thus records the covenant title and the awe of Moses — teaching that God made himself known as the covenant God of the fathers, and Moses hid his face in holy fear.
7:
"And the LORD said, I have surely seen the affliction of my people which are in Egypt, and have heard their cry by reason of their taskmasters; for I know their sorrows;" The witnesses note God declaring he has seen the affliction, heard the cry, and known the sorrows of his people. Gill notes "in seeing I have seen" denotes not only the certainty but the clear, distinct sight he had of their affliction, with sympathy and an affectionate concern, and a fixed determination to deliver them. The witnesses note the LORD saying he had surely seen Israel's affliction, heard their cry, and known their sorrows; the tender compassion of God for his suffering people — that God reveals his fatherly heart toward Israel: "I have surely seen the affliction of my people... and have heard their cry... for I know their sorrows"; the witnesses note the emphasis, "I have surely seen" (literally "in seeing I have seen"), which marks not the certainty only but the clear, distinct, and feeling sight God had of their misery, regarding it with "sympathy" and "affectionate concern"; the witnesses note the threefold word of compassion, "seen the affliction... heard their cry... I know their sorrows" — that God beheld their bondage, heard their groaning under "their taskmasters," and inwardly knew and felt their grief; the witnesses note the comfort the commentators draw, that God is not indifferent to his people's sufferings, but sees, hears, and knows them with a tender and saving regard; the witnesses note the owning of them, "my people," the covenant tie that moved his pity; so that God declares his tender compassion for his afflicted people; for I know their sorrows. The witnesses note I have surely seen the affliction of my people which are in Egypt, and have heard their cry by reason of their taskmasters. The witnesses note for I know their sorrows.

The witnesses note God's tender compassion — "I have surely seen the affliction of my people... I know their sorrows" — beholding their bondage, hearing their cry, inwardly feeling their grief; God is not indifferent to his people's sufferings, but sees, hears, and knows with a saving regard.

So the witnesses make the verse God's tender compassion for his afflicted people. The lesson the expositors draw is that God saw, heard, and knew Israel's affliction with sympathy and a settled purpose to deliver, owning them as his people. The witnesses note the surely seen: "I have surely seen the affliction" — Gill notes a clear, feeling sight; the surely seen; the a clear sight; the feeling; the sympathy; the concern. The expositors observe the heard their cry: "have heard their cry" — their groaning under taskmasters; the heard their cry; the their groaning; the the taskmasters; the the cry; the heard. The witnesses press the know their sorrows: "I know their sorrows" — inwardly felt their grief; the know their sorrows; the inwardly; the felt; the their grief; the known. The expositors draw the lesson on the not indifferent: God not indifferent to suffering; the not indifferent; the not indifferent; the suffering; the sees; the hears. The witnesses observe the saving regard: with a tender, saving regard; the saving regard; the tender; the saving; the regard; the determined. The expositors note the my people: "my people" — the covenant tie; the my people; the my people; the the covenant; the the tie; the moved pity. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I have surely seen the affliction of my people which are in Egypt and have heard their cry by reason of their taskmasters, the for I know their sorrows, and the compassion. The verse thus records God's tender compassion — teaching that God saw, heard, and knew Israel's affliction with sympathy and a settled purpose to deliver.
8:
"And I am come down to deliver them out of the hand of the Egyptians, and to bring them up out of that land unto a good land and a large, unto a land flowing with milk and honey..." The witnesses note God coming down to deliver Israel and bring them to a good land flowing with milk and honey. Gill notes God's coming down is spoken consistent with his omnipresence, used when he gives some eminent display of his power or goodness, as here in delivering his people. The witnesses note God saying he was come down to deliver Israel from the Egyptians and bring them to a good and large land flowing with milk and honey; the gracious purpose of deliverance and the promise of the good land — that God declares both the rescue and the inheritance: "I am come down to deliver them out of the hand of the Egyptians... unto a good land and a large, unto a land flowing with milk and honey"; the witnesses note the manner of speech, "I am come down" — that God, who is everywhere and cannot remove from place to place, is yet said to "come down" when he gives some great display of his power and goodness, as here to save; the witnesses note the double mercy, "to deliver them out of the hand of the Egyptians" (the rescue from bondage) and "to bring them up... unto a good land and a large" (the inheritance promised the fathers); the witnesses note the rich description, "a land flowing with milk and honey" — a fruitful, abundant land, fitly figuring also the heavenly Canaan and the riches of the gospel; the witnesses note the faithfulness of God, performing to the seed the promise sworn to Abraham; so that God comes down to deliver and to give the good land; unto a land flowing with milk and honey. The witnesses note I am come down to deliver them out of the hand of the Egyptians. The witnesses note unto a good land and a large, unto a land flowing with milk and honey.

The witnesses note God's gracious purpose — "come down to deliver them... unto a good land... flowing with milk and honey" — the double mercy of rescue from bondage and the promised inheritance; the abundant land fitly figuring also the heavenly Canaan and the riches of the gospel.

So the witnesses make the verse God's purpose of deliverance and the good land. The lesson the expositors draw is that God came down to deliver Israel from Egypt and bring them to the good land of promise, faithful to the oath sworn to Abraham. The witnesses note the come down: "I am come down" — Gill notes a display of power and goodness; the come down; the a display; the power; the goodness; the to save. The expositors observe the to deliver: "to deliver them out of the hand of the Egyptians" — rescue from bondage; the to deliver; the from bondage. The witnesses press the a good land: "a good land and a large" — the promised inheritance; the a good land; the promised. The expositors draw the lesson on the milk and honey: "flowing with milk and honey" — fruitful and abundant; the milk and honey; the fruitful; the abundant; the the land; the rich. The witnesses observe the heavenly Canaan: figuring the heavenly Canaan; the heavenly Canaan; the the gospel. The expositors note the faithfulness: God faithful to the oath; the faithfulness; the the oath; the to Abraham. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the unto a good land and a large unto a land flowing with milk and honey, and the deliverance. The verse thus records God's purpose of deliverance — teaching that God came down to deliver Israel and bring them to the good land, faithful to his oath.
9:
"Now therefore, behold, the cry of the children of Israel is come unto me: and I have also seen the oppression wherewith the Egyptians oppress them." The witnesses note God repeating that Israel's cry has come up to him and he has seen their oppression. Gill notes the cry of the children of Israel is come unto him, and he had seen the oppression with which the Egyptians oppressed them. The witnesses note God declaring again that the cry of Israel had come to him and he had seen the oppression of the Egyptians; the renewed assurance that God had heard and seen, the ground of his sending Moses — that God states again the cause that moves him: "the cry of the children of Israel is come unto me: and I have also seen the oppression wherewith the Egyptians oppress them"; the witnesses note the repetition (vv.7, 9), pressing the certainty that God had truly heard and seen, that "the cry of the children of Israel is come unto me," ascending to heaven and not unheeded; the witnesses note the sight of the wrong, "I have also seen the oppression wherewith the Egyptians oppress them" — that God beheld the cruel oppression laid on his people, the heavy bondage and bitter usage; the witnesses note that this declaration is the ground laid for the commission that follows (v.10), God's compassion moving him to send a deliverer; the witnesses note the comfort, that the cries of the oppressed do come up to God, who will avenge his elect; so that God's hearing and seeing ground his sending of Moses; the oppression wherewith the Egyptians oppress them. The witnesses note the cry of the children of Israel is come unto me. The witnesses note and I have also seen the oppression wherewith the Egyptians oppress them.

The witnesses note God declaring again that Israel's cry had come to him and he had seen the Egyptians' oppression — pressing the certainty that the cries of the oppressed do come up to God, the ground of his sending a deliverer; God will avenge his elect.

So the witnesses make the verse the renewed assurance that grounds the commission. The lesson the expositors draw is that God had truly heard Israel's cry and seen their oppression, the ground laid for sending Moses to deliver them. The witnesses note the cry come unto me: "the cry... is come unto me" — Gill notes ascended to heaven; the cry come unto me; the ascended; the to heaven; the not unheeded; the heard. The expositors observe the seen the oppression: "I have also seen the oppression" — the cruel bondage; the seen the oppression; the the cruel bondage; the the wrong; the heavy; the bitter. The witnesses press the the repetition: pressed again from v.7; the the repetition; the repeated; the v.7; the the certainty; the truly. The expositors draw the lesson on the ground of commission: the ground for sending Moses; the ground of commission; the the ground; the for sending; the Moses; the the deliverer. The witnesses observe the cries come up: the cries of the oppressed come up to God; the cries come up; the the cries; the the oppressed; the come up; the to God. The expositors note the avenge his elect: God will avenge his elect; the avenge his elect; the avenge; the his elect; the comfort; the the oppressed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the the cry of the children of Israel is come unto me, the and I have also seen the oppression wherewith the Egyptians oppress them, and the assurance. The verse thus records the renewed assurance — teaching that God had heard Israel's cry and seen their oppression, the ground for sending Moses.
10:
"Come now therefore, and I will send thee unto Pharaoh, that thou mayest bring forth my people the children of Israel out of Egypt." The witnesses note God commissioning Moses to go to Pharaoh and bring Israel out. John Calvin notes that after God had furnished his servant with promises to engage him more cheerfully, he now adds commands and calls him to undertake the office to which he is designed. The witnesses note God bidding Moses come and be sent to Pharaoh, to bring forth his people Israel out of Egypt; the great commission of Moses, joined to God's gracious promises — that God, having shown his compassion, now lays the charge: "Come now therefore, and I will send thee unto Pharaoh, that thou mayest bring forth my people the children of Israel out of Egypt"; the witnesses note the order Calvin marks, that God first "furnished his servant with promises to engage him more cheerfully," and then "adds commands," so that the promise goes before the precept, the best encouragement to duty; the witnesses note the charge, "I will send thee unto Pharaoh" — that Moses must leave his flock and family and go to the mighty king to demand Israel's release; the witnesses note the end of the sending, "that thou mayest bring forth my people the children of Israel out of Egypt" — that Moses should be God's instrument to lead out the covenant people; the witnesses note the honour and the weight of so great a commission, God working his deliverance by a man; so that God commissions Moses to bring Israel out; bring forth my people the children of Israel out of Egypt. The witnesses note Come now therefore, and I will send thee unto Pharaoh. The witnesses note that thou mayest bring forth my people the children of Israel out of Egypt.

The witnesses note God's great commission — "I will send thee unto Pharaoh... bring forth my people" — the promise going before the precept (the best encouragement to duty); God working his deliverance by a man, Moses his instrument to lead out the covenant people.

So the witnesses make the verse the great commission of Moses. The lesson the expositors draw is that God, having shown his compassion and given his promises, charged Moses to go to Pharaoh and bring forth his people, the promise before the precept. The witnesses note the come now: "Come now therefore" — Calvin notes the call to office; the come now; the Calvin; the the call; the the office; the designed. The expositors observe the promise before precept: the promise goes before the command; the promise before precept; the the promise; the the command; the engages; the cheerfully. The verse thus records the great commission of Moses — teaching that God charged Moses to bring forth his people, the promise before the precept.
11:
"And Moses said unto God, Who am I, that I should go unto Pharaoh, and that I should bring forth the children of Israel out of Egypt?" The witnesses note Moses' first objection, a sense of his own unfitness. Gill notes Moses pleaded he was a private person, an exile, a poor shepherd, unknown to Pharaoh, while Pharaoh was a great king with numerous forces. The witnesses note Moses asking, "Who am I, that I should go unto Pharaoh, and that I should bring forth the children of Israel?"; the diffidence of Moses, a sense of his own weakness and unfitness for the task — that Moses shrinks back, conscious of his lowliness: "Who am I, that I should go unto Pharaoh, and that I should bring forth the children of Israel out of Egypt?"; the witnesses note the ground of his hesitation, that Moses felt himself "a private person, an exile in a foreign country, a poor shepherd," unknown to and without interest with Pharaoh, "a great king" with mighty forces, and so unequal to so great a task; the witnesses note the mingled humility and weakness in this, that Moses, who forty years before had been forward (2:11-14), is now so abased that he shrinks from the work; the witnesses note that such a sense of one's own insufficiency is fitting in itself, yet must not be suffered to refuse God's call, for the sufficiency is of God, not of the man; the witnesses note that God meets the objection not by exalting Moses but by promising his own presence (v.12); so that Moses pleads his own unfitness for the task; that I should bring forth the children of Israel out of Egypt. The witnesses note Who am I, that I should go unto Pharaoh. The witnesses note and that I should bring forth the children of Israel out of Egypt?

The witnesses note Moses shrinking back — "Who am I?" — conscious of his lowliness, a poor shepherd unequal to the mighty king; a sense of insufficiency fitting in itself, yet not to refuse God's call, for the sufficiency is of God, not the man.

So the witnesses make the verse Moses pleading his own unfitness. The lesson the expositors draw is that Moses, sensible of his weakness, shrank from the task, a humility that must not refuse God's call, the sufficiency being of God. The witnesses note the who am I: "Who am I, that I should go unto Pharaoh" — Gill notes a poor shepherd; the who am I; the a poor shepherd; the an exile; the unknown; the lowly. The expositors observe the unequal to the king: unequal to the mighty king; the unequal to the king; the unequal; the the king; the mighty; the forces. The witnesses press the diffidence: the diffidence of Moses; the diffidence; the diffidence; the weakness; the unfitness; the shrinks. The expositors draw the lesson on the once forward: once forward, now abased; the once forward; the forward; the now abased; the forty years; the shrinks. The witnesses observe the fitting humility: a fitting sense of insufficiency; the fitting humility; the fitting; the insufficiency; the in itself; the humble. The expositors note the not refuse the call: yet not to refuse God's call; the not refuse the call; the not refuse; the the call; the the sufficiency; the of God. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Who am I that I should go unto Pharaoh, the and that I should bring forth the children of Israel out of Egypt, and the objection. The verse thus records Moses pleading his unfitness — teaching that Moses shrank from the task, a humility that must not refuse God's call, the sufficiency being of God.
12:
"And he said, Certainly I will be with thee; and this shall be a token unto thee, that I have sent thee: When thou hast brought forth the people out of Egypt, ye shall serve God upon this mountain." The witnesses note God answering Moses' fear with the promise of his presence and a token. Keil and Delitzsch note God said, Certainly I will be with thee, and gave him a sign that he had sent him: when he had brought forth the people, they should serve God upon that mountain. The witnesses note God promising, "Certainly I will be with thee," and giving a token, that they should serve God on that mountain; the all-sufficient answer to Moses' weakness, the presence of God and the pledge of success — that God meets the "Who am I?" not with Moses' worth but with his own presence: "Certainly I will be with thee; and this shall be a token unto thee... ye shall serve God upon this mountain"; the witnesses note the heart of the answer, "Certainly I will be with thee" — that it matters not who Moses is, for God will be with him to "encourage, strengthen, protect, defend," and prosper him; the presence of God is the strength of the weak servant; the witnesses note the token given, "this shall be a token unto thee, that I have sent thee: When thou hast brought forth the people out of Egypt, ye shall serve God upon this mountain" — a sign to confirm his faith, that the very place of his calling (Horeb/Sinai) should be the place where the redeemed people would worship God, the deliverance assured beforehand; the witnesses note that the token was a thing of faith, to be believed before it was seen, resting on God's word; the witnesses note that God's presence and promise are enough to silence every plea of weakness; so that God answers Moses' fear with his presence and a token; ye shall serve God upon this mountain. The witnesses note Certainly I will be with thee. The witnesses note When thou hast brought forth the people out of Egypt, ye shall serve God upon this mountain.

The witnesses note God meeting Moses' weakness not with his worth but with his own presence — "Certainly I will be with thee" — and a token of faith, that the redeemed people would worship God on that very mountain; God's presence is the strength of the weak servant.

So the witnesses make the verse God's all-sufficient answer to Moses' fear. The lesson the expositors draw is that God answered the "Who am I?" with the promise of his presence and a token, his presence being enough to silence every plea of weakness. The witnesses note the I will be with thee: "Certainly I will be with thee" — Keil notes to strengthen and prosper; the I will be with thee; the the presence. The witnesses press the strength of the weak: God's presence the strength of the weak; the strength of the weak; the the servant; the upheld. The expositors draw the lesson on the the token: "this shall be a token unto thee" — a sign to confirm faith; the the token; the confirm; the faith. The witnesses observe the serve on this mountain: "ye shall serve God upon this mountain" — the place of worship; the serve on this mountain; the Horeb; the the redeemed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the When thou hast brought forth the people out of Egypt ye shall serve God upon this mountain, and the answer. The verse thus records God's all-sufficient answer — teaching that God answered Moses' fear with his presence and a token, his presence enough to silence every plea of weakness.
13:
"And Moses said unto God, Behold, when I come unto the children of Israel, and shall say unto them, The God of your fathers hath sent me unto you; and they shall say to me, What is his name? what shall I say unto them?" The witnesses note Moses asking what name he should give when Israel asks who has sent him. Gill notes Moses, having received satisfaction to his first objection, raises another difficulty, that the people might ask by what name God had revealed himself. The witnesses note Moses asking God what he should answer if Israel asked the name of the God who had sent him; the desire of Moses to have a clear warrant, and the occasion of the great revelation of the name — that Moses seeks the name to give: "when I come unto the children of Israel... and they shall say to me, What is his name? what shall I say unto them?"; the witnesses note the prudence of Moses, wishing his commission to be plain and firm, that he might tell the people exactly who had sent him; the witnesses note the likely reason of the question, that the people, in their long bondage, might ask by what particular name God now made himself known, or seek some fresh token of the God of their fathers; the witnesses note that this honest inquiry became the occasion of God's revealing his great and proper name; the witnesses note that it is right to seek a clear warrant for God's work, that we may speak with authority; so that Moses asks the name, occasioning the great revelation; what shall I say unto them? The witnesses note when I come unto the children of Israel... The God of your fathers hath sent me unto you. The witnesses note and they shall say to me, What is his name? what shall I say unto them?

The witnesses note Moses asking what name to give when Israel asks who has sent him — wishing his commission plain and firm; an honest inquiry that became the occasion of God's revealing his great and proper name.

So the witnesses make the verse Moses seeking the name, the occasion of the great revelation. The lesson the expositors draw is that Moses, wishing a clear warrant, asked what name to give Israel, occasioning God's revealing of his proper name. The witnesses note the when I come: "when I come unto the children of Israel" — Gill notes a second difficulty; the when I come; the a second difficulty; the the people; the may ask; the the name. The expositors observe the what is his name: "What is his name?" — the people's likely question; the what is his name; the the question; the the people; the the name; the make known. 14:
"And God said unto Moses, I AM THAT I AM: and he said, Thus shalt thou say unto the children of Israel, I AM hath sent me unto you." The witnesses note the great revelation of the divine name, "I AM THAT I AM." Gill notes this signifies the real being of God, his self-existence, that he is the Being of beings, his eternity and immutability, and his constancy and faithfulness in fulfilling his promises, for it includes all time past, present, and to come. The witnesses note God answering Moses, "I AM THAT I AM," and bidding him say, "I AM hath sent me unto you"; the supreme revelation of God's name, declaring his self-existence, eternity, and unchanging faithfulness — that God reveals himself by the name above all names: "I AM THAT I AM... Thus shalt thou say unto the children of Israel, I AM hath sent me unto you"; the witnesses note the depth of the name, that "I AM THAT I AM" declares "the real being of God, his self-existence," that he alone truly is, "the Being of beings," depending on none, the source of all; the witnesses note that it denotes his "eternity and immutability," for the name "includes all time, past, present, and to come" — he who was, and is, and is to come, ever the same; the witnesses note the comfort drawn for Israel, that this unchanging, ever-living God would surely keep his promises and deliver them, "his constancy and faithfulness in fulfilling his promises"; the witnesses note the great fulfilment the commentators mark, that the Lord Jesus took this very name to himself, "Before Abraham was, I AM" (John 8:58), declaring his eternal Godhead; so that God reveals his proper name, the self-existent and faithful I AM; I AM hath sent me unto you. The witnesses note And God said unto Moses, I AM THAT I AM. The witnesses note Thus shalt thou say unto the children of Israel, I AM hath sent me unto you.

The witnesses note the supreme name "I AM THAT I AM" — declaring God's self-existence, eternity, and unchanging faithfulness, including all time past, present, and to come; the ground of Israel's hope that the ever-living God would keep his promises, and the name Christ took to himself ("Before Abraham was, I AM").

So the witnesses make the verse the great revelation of the divine name. The lesson the expositors draw is that God revealed himself as "I AM THAT I AM," the self-existent, eternal, and unchanging God, faithful to fulfil his promises. The witnesses note the I AM THAT I AM: "I AM THAT I AM" — Gill notes the real being of God; the I AM THAT I AM; the the real being; the self-existence; the the Being of beings; the the source. The witnesses observe the comfort for Israel: the comfort that he would deliver; the comfort for Israel; the the ever-living. The expositors note the Christ took it: Christ took this name, "I AM"; the Christ took it; the Before Abraham; the his Godhead. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the Thus shalt thou say unto the children of Israel I AM hath sent me unto you, and the great name. The verse thus records the revelation of the divine name — teaching that God revealed himself as the self-existent, eternal, and faithful I AM.
15:
"And God said moreover unto Moses, Thus shalt thou say unto the children of Israel, The LORD God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, hath sent me unto you: this is my name for ever, and this is my memorial unto all generations." The witnesses note God giving his memorial name, the LORD God of the fathers, for ever. Adam Clarke notes the name here referred to is Jehovah Elohim, the Lord God, the name by which God is known as the fountain of being and the keeper of covenant. The witnesses note God bidding Moses say the LORD God of the fathers, of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob, had sent him, this being his name for ever and his memorial to all generations; the joining of the eternal name to the covenant name, God's everlasting memorial — that God knits "I AM" to "the God of the fathers": "The LORD God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, the God of Isaac, and the God of Jacob, hath sent me unto you: this is my name for ever"; the witnesses note the two names joined, the proper name "the LORD" (Jehovah, the self-existent I AM of v.14) and the covenant name "the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob," so that the eternal God is also the faithful covenant-keeper of the fathers; the witnesses note the permanence, "this is my name for ever, and this is my memorial unto all generations" — that this name, Jehovah, should be the abiding name and memorial by which God would be known and remembered through all ages; the witnesses note the comfort, that the unchanging I AM is the same God who covenanted with the fathers and would keep covenant with their seed; the witnesses note that the name is God's chosen memorial, by which his people should call upon and remember him for ever; so that God gives his everlasting memorial name; this is my memorial unto all generations. The witnesses note The LORD God of your fathers... hath sent me unto you. The witnesses note this is my name for ever, and this is my memorial unto all generations.

The witnesses note God joining the eternal name "the LORD" (Jehovah, the I AM) to the covenant name "the God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob" — his abiding name and memorial to all generations; the unchanging I AM is the same God who covenanted with the fathers and keeps covenant with their seed.

So the witnesses make the verse the everlasting memorial name. The lesson the expositors draw is that God joined his eternal name to the covenant name, his abiding memorial through all generations, the I AM faithful to the fathers' covenant. The witnesses press the my name for ever: "this is my name for ever" — the abiding name; the my name for ever; the known. The witnesses observe the the same God: the I AM the same covenant God; the the same God; the the I AM; the the covenant. The expositors note the keep covenant: faithful to keep covenant; the keep covenant; the the seed; the faithful. The verse thus records the everlasting memorial name — teaching that God joined his eternal name to the covenant name, his abiding memorial through all generations.
16:
"Go, and gather the elders of Israel together, and say unto them, The LORD God of your fathers, the God of Abraham, of Isaac, and of Jacob, appeared unto me, saying, I have surely visited you, and seen that which is done to you in Egypt:" The witnesses note God sending Moses to gather the elders and tell them God had visited them. Matthew Henry notes Moses is here instructed what to say to the elders of Israel, the heads of the tribes or families. The witnesses note God bidding Moses gather the elders of Israel and tell them the LORD had appeared to him, saying he had surely visited them and seen their affliction; the charge to carry the good news of God's visitation to the elders — that Moses is sent first to the leaders of the people: "gather the elders of Israel together, and say unto them, The LORD God of your fathers... appeared unto me, saying, I have surely visited you"; the witnesses note the means appointed, "gather the elders of Israel together" — the heads of the tribes and families, that through them the word might come to all the people, an orderly way; the witnesses note the message of comfort, "The LORD God of your fathers... appeared unto me" — that the covenant God of Abraham, Isaac, and Jacob had now revealed himself and was about to act; the witnesses note the gracious visitation, "I have surely visited you, and seen that which is done to you in Egypt" — that God had come to look upon and to help them, "surely visited" them in mercy, having seen their oppression; the witnesses note that this visitation was the dawn of their deliverance, the long-awaited remembrance of God's promise; so that Moses is sent to tell the elders of God's visitation; seen that which is done to you in Egypt. The witnesses note Go, and gather the elders of Israel together. The witnesses note I have surely visited you, and seen that which is done to you in Egypt.

The witnesses note God sending Moses to gather the elders and tell them the covenant God had appeared and "surely visited" them — the good news of God's gracious visitation in mercy, the dawn of their deliverance, the long-awaited remembrance of his promise.

So the witnesses make the verse the message of God's visitation to the elders. The lesson the expositors draw is that Moses was sent to gather the elders and bring the good news that the covenant God had visited his people in mercy, the dawn of deliverance. The witnesses note the gather the elders: "gather the elders of Israel together" — Henry notes the heads of the tribes; the gather the elders; the Henry; the the heads; the the tribes; the orderly. The expositors observe the through them to all: through them to all the people; the through them to all; the the word. The witnesses press the the LORD appeared: "The LORD... appeared unto me" — the covenant God; the the LORD appeared; the about to act. The expositors draw the lesson on the surely visited: "I have surely visited you" — come in mercy; the surely visited; the visited; the in mercy; the to help; the remembered. The witnesses observe the seen the affliction: "seen that which is done to you" — beheld their oppression; the seen the affliction; the their oppression; the beheld. The expositors note the the dawn: the dawn of their deliverance; the the dawn; the deliverance; the the promise. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I have surely visited you and seen that which is done to you in Egypt, and the visitation. The verse thus records the message of visitation — teaching that Moses was sent to bring the good news that the covenant God had visited his people in mercy.
17:
"And I have said, I will bring you up out of the affliction of Egypt unto the land of the Canaanites... unto a land flowing with milk and honey." The witnesses note God's resolved promise to bring Israel up out of affliction to the good land. Keil and Delitzsch note God said, I will bring you up out of the affliction of Egypt unto the land of the Canaanites and the other nations, a land flowing with milk and honey. The witnesses note God declaring his settled resolve to bring Israel out of affliction to the land flowing with milk and honey; the renewed promise of deliverance and inheritance, God's fixed purpose — that God seals to the elders the promise of the good land: "I have said, I will bring you up out of the affliction of Egypt... unto a land flowing with milk and honey"; the witnesses note the settledness, "I have said" — that God had "resolved in his own mind, and declared it," his fixed and unchangeable purpose to save them; the witnesses note the double mercy again, "bring you up out of the affliction of Egypt" (the deliverance from bondage) and "unto a land flowing with milk and honey" (the good inheritance of Canaan, then held by the Canaanites and the other nations named); the witnesses note the certainty of the promise, resting on the word of the unchanging I AM, who cannot fail to perform; the witnesses note the comfort to the afflicted people, that their misery should end in a land of plenty and rest; the witnesses note the type the commentators draw, of the believer's deliverance from sin's bondage to the heavenly inheritance; so that God seals the promise of deliverance and the good land; unto a land flowing with milk and honey. The witnesses note I will bring you up out of the affliction of Egypt. The witnesses note unto a land flowing with milk and honey.

The witnesses note God's settled resolve — "I will bring you up out of the affliction of Egypt... unto a land flowing with milk and honey" — his fixed and unchangeable purpose to save; the double mercy of deliverance and inheritance, resting on the word of the unchanging I AM.

So the witnesses make the verse God's resolved promise of deliverance and inheritance. The lesson the expositors draw is that God declared his fixed purpose to bring Israel out of bondage to the good land, certain because it rests on the word of the unchanging I AM. The witnesses note the I have said: "I have said" — Gill notes a fixed purpose; the I have said; the a fixed purpose; the resolved; the declared; the unchangeable. The expositors observe the bring you up: "bring you up out of the affliction" — deliverance from bondage; the bring you up; the deliverance; the from bondage; the affliction; the saved. The witnesses press the milk and honey: "a land flowing with milk and honey" — the good inheritance; the milk and honey; the the inheritance; the Canaan; the the good land; the plenty. The expositors draw the lesson on the certainty: certain on God's word; the certainty; the certain; the God's word; the the I AM; the cannot fail. The witnesses observe the comfort to the afflicted: comfort to the afflicted people; the comfort to the afflicted; the comfort; the the afflicted; the rest; the plenty. The expositors note the the type: a type of the believer's deliverance; the the type; the from sin; the the heavenly inheritance. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the unto a land flowing with milk and honey, and the resolved promise. The verse thus records God's resolved promise — teaching that God declared his fixed purpose to bring Israel out of bondage to the good land.
18:
"And they shall hearken to thy voice: and thou shalt come, thou and the elders of Israel, unto the king of Egypt, and ye shall say unto him, The LORD God of the Hebrews hath met with us: and now let us go, we beseech thee, three days' journey into the wilderness, that we may sacrifice to the LORD our God." The witnesses note God promising the elders would hearken, and directing the request to Pharaoh. Adam Clarke notes they shall hearken to thy voice, a promise that the elders would give credit to his commission. The witnesses note God promising that the elders would hearken, and bidding Moses with them ask Pharaoh leave for a three days' journey to sacrifice; the assurance of the elders' faith and the first modest request to Pharaoh — that God foretells success with the elders and directs the suit to the king: "they shall hearken to thy voice... ye shall say unto him, The LORD God of the Hebrews hath met with us: and now let us go... three days' journey into the wilderness, that we may sacrifice"; the witnesses note the encouragement, "they shall hearken to thy voice" — that, unlike his first rejection (2:14), the elders would now "give credit to his commission," attend, and obey, God going before to open their hearts; the witnesses note the manner of the request to Pharaoh, "The LORD God of the Hebrews hath met with us: and now let us go... three days' journey... that we may sacrifice to the LORD our God" — a modest and reasonable suit, asking leave to go and worship, that Pharaoh's refusal might be without excuse; the witnesses note the wisdom of the demand, framed to lay bare Pharaoh's hard heart, since even so small a request he would deny; the witnesses note that God orders the means as well as the end; so that the elders hearken, and the suit is laid before Pharaoh; that we may sacrifice to the LORD our God. The witnesses note they shall hearken to thy voice. The witnesses note let us go, we beseech thee, three days' journey into the wilderness, that we may sacrifice to the LORD our God.

The witnesses note God promising the elders would now hearken (unlike Moses' first rejection), and directing the modest suit to Pharaoh — leave to go three days' journey to sacrifice; a reasonable request framed to lay bare Pharaoh's hard heart, since even so small a thing he would deny.

So the witnesses make the verse the elders' faith and the first suit to Pharaoh. The lesson the expositors draw is that God promised the elders would hearken and directed a modest request to Pharaoh, framed to leave his refusal without excuse. The witnesses note the hearken to thy voice: "they shall hearken to thy voice" — Clarke notes they would credit his commission; the hearken to thy voice; the credit; the obey. The witnesses press the the LORD met with us: "The LORD God of the Hebrews hath met with us" — the ground of the suit; the the LORD met with us; the the Hebrews. The expositors draw the lesson on the three days' journey: "three days' journey... that we may sacrifice" — a modest request; the three days' journey; the to worship; the leave. The expositors note the orders the means: God orders the means; the orders the means; the the end. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the let us go we beseech thee three days' journey into the wilderness that we may sacrifice to the LORD our God, and the suit. The verse thus records the elders' faith and the suit to Pharaoh — teaching that God promised the elders would hearken and directed a modest request framed to leave Pharaoh's refusal without excuse.
19:
"And I am sure that the king of Egypt will not let you go, no, not by a mighty hand." The witnesses note God foretelling that Pharaoh would not let Israel go, even under a mighty hand. Gill notes though so reasonable a request was made, it would not be granted; this is observed that they might not be discouraged when he should refuse to dismiss them, which the omniscient God knew. The witnesses note God declaring he was sure the king of Egypt would not let them go, not even by a mighty hand; the foreknowledge of Pharaoh's hard refusal, told beforehand to forewarn the people — that God reveals the resistance to come: "I am sure that the king of Egypt will not let you go, no, not by a mighty hand"; the witnesses note God's certain foreknowledge, "I am sure," that the omniscient God knew Pharaoh's heart and foretold that the king "will not let you go," for all the reasonableness of the request; the witnesses note the force of "no, not by a mighty hand," variously taken — that Pharaoh would not yield even when a mighty hand (God's, in the plagues) was stretched against him, until it was fully shown; or that he would not let them go except by a mighty hand compelling him; the witnesses note the purpose of the warning, that the people "might not be discouraged" when Pharaoh refused, knowing it was foretold and part of God's plan; the witnesses note that God's foreknowledge of opposition does not hinder but forwards his purpose, his power being made known in the overcoming; so that God foretells Pharaoh's hard refusal; no, not by a mighty hand. The witnesses note And I am sure that the king of Egypt will not let you go. The witnesses note no, not by a mighty hand.

The witnesses note God foretelling, by his certain foreknowledge, that Pharaoh would not let Israel go even under a mighty hand — the warning given that the people might not be discouraged when he refused, knowing it was foretold and part of God's plan.

So the witnesses make the verse the foreknowledge of Pharaoh's refusal. The lesson the expositors draw is that God foretold, by his certain knowledge of Pharaoh's heart, that the king would not let Israel go, forewarning the people lest they be discouraged. The witnesses note the I am sure: "I am sure" — Gill notes the omniscient God knew; the I am sure; the the omniscient God; the knew; the certain; the foreknowledge. The expositors observe the will not let you go: "will not let you go" — Pharaoh's hard refusal; the will not let you go; the the refusal; the Pharaoh; the hard; the for all the request. The witnesses press the not by a mighty hand: "no, not by a mighty hand" — would not yield; the not by a mighty hand; the the mighty hand; the would not yield; the the plagues; the compelled. The expositors draw the lesson on the not discouraged: that they be not discouraged; the not discouraged; the not discouraged; the the people; the foretold; the the plan. The witnesses observe the foreknowledge forwards: foreknowledge forwards God's purpose; the foreknowledge forwards; the foreknowledge; the forwards; the his purpose; the overcoming. The expositors note the power made known: God's power made known in overcoming; the power made known; the power; the made known; the overcoming; the the plagues. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the And I am sure that the king of Egypt will not let you go, the no not by a mighty hand, and the foreknowledge. The verse thus records the foreknowledge of Pharaoh's refusal — teaching that God foretold the king's resistance, forewarning the people lest they be discouraged.
20:
"And I will stretch out my hand, and smite Egypt with all my wonders which I will do in the midst thereof: and after that he will let you go." The witnesses note God's promise to smite Egypt with wonders so that Pharaoh would let Israel go. Charles Ellicott notes hands are ascribed to God after the manner of men, denoting his power put forth in action. The witnesses note God promising to stretch out his hand and smite Egypt with all his wonders, after which Pharaoh would let them go; the assurance that God's mighty wonders would break Pharaoh's resistance — that God answers Pharaoh's hardness with his almighty power: "I will stretch out my hand, and smite Egypt with all my wonders... and after that he will let you go"; the witnesses note the figure, "I will stretch out my hand," God's power put forth in action, "spoken after the manner of men" (Ellicott), the hand of the Almighty raised against Egypt; the witnesses note the means, "smite Egypt with all my wonders which I will do in the midst thereof" — the plagues, the great wonders God would work in Egypt to humble Pharaoh and judge his gods; the witnesses note the certain issue, "and after that he will let you go" — that all Pharaoh's resistance should at last be broken, and he would be forced to let Israel go, God's power prevailing over the proudest enemy; the witnesses note that what man's reason could not win, God's mighty hand would surely accomplish; so that God's wonders would break Pharaoh's resistance; after that he will let you go. The witnesses note I will stretch out my hand, and smite Egypt with all my wonders which I will do in the midst thereof. The witnesses note and after that he will let you go.

The witnesses note God answering Pharaoh's hardness with his almighty power — "I will stretch out my hand, and smite Egypt with all my wonders" — the plagues that would humble Pharaoh and break his resistance, so that what man's reason could not win, God's mighty hand would accomplish.

So the witnesses make the verse God's wonders breaking Pharaoh's resistance. The lesson the expositors draw is that God would stretch out his hand and smite Egypt with wonders, breaking Pharaoh's hardness so that he must let Israel go. The witnesses note the stretch out my hand: "I will stretch out my hand" — Ellicott notes God's power in action; the stretch out my hand; the Ellicott; the God's power; the in action; the after men. The expositors observe the smite Egypt: "smite Egypt with all my wonders" — the plagues; the smite Egypt; the the plagues; the the wonders; the in the midst; the judge. The witnesses press the all my wonders: the great wonders in Egypt; the all my wonders; the the wonders; the great; the in Egypt; the humble. The expositors draw the lesson on the he will let you go: "after that he will let you go" — resistance broken; the he will let you go; the resistance broken; the forced; the at last; the prevail. The witnesses observe the over the proudest: God prevails over the proudest enemy; the over the proudest; the God prevails; the the proudest; the the enemy; the broken. The expositors note the what reason could not: what reason could not win; the what reason could not; the reason; the could not; the the mighty hand; the accomplish. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I will stretch out my hand and smite Egypt with all my wonders which I will do in the midst thereof, the and after that he will let you go, and the wonders. The verse thus records God's wonders breaking Pharaoh's resistance — teaching that God would smite Egypt with wonders so that Pharaoh must let Israel go.
21:
"And I will give this people favour in the sight of the Egyptians: and it shall come to pass, that, when ye go, ye shall not go empty:" The witnesses note God promising Israel favour with the Egyptians, that they would not go empty. Gill notes God would give the Israelites favour in the sight of the Egyptians, who would be ready to do or bestow anything for them, or lend what they desired. The witnesses note God promising to give his people favour with the Egyptians, so that they should not go out empty; the gracious promise that the delivered people should depart enriched, not destitute — that God provides that Israel leave Egypt with their wages: "I will give this people favour in the sight of the Egyptians: and... when ye go, ye shall not go empty"; the witnesses note the turning of hearts, "I will give this people favour in the sight of the Egyptians" — that God, who holds all hearts, would so incline the Egyptians (humbled by the plagues) that they should be willing, even eager, to give to the Israelites; the witnesses note the promise, "ye shall not go empty" — that the people, after long years of unrequited toil, should not depart bare and destitute, but laden with goods, a kind of recompense for their hard bondage; the witnesses note the justice and mercy in this, that God would see his people righted and provided for, sending them out with substance; the witnesses note that God can turn the hearts of enemies to favour his own when he pleases; so that God promises Israel favour and a full departure; ye shall not go empty. The witnesses note I will give this people favour in the sight of the Egyptians. The witnesses note that, when ye go, ye shall not go empty.

The witnesses note God promising to give Israel favour with the Egyptians, so that they should not go out empty but laden with goods — a recompense for their long unrequited toil; God can turn the hearts of enemies to favour his own when he pleases.

So the witnesses make the verse the promise of favour and a full departure. The lesson the expositors draw is that God would incline the Egyptians to favour Israel, that the delivered people should depart enriched, not destitute, a recompense for their bondage. The witnesses note the give favour: "I will give this people favour" — Gill notes God inclines the Egyptians; the give favour; the inclines; the the Egyptians; the willing; the to give. The expositors observe the holds all hearts: God holds all hearts; the holds all hearts; the holds; the all hearts; the turns; the inclines. The witnesses press the not go empty: "ye shall not go empty" — laden with goods; the not go empty; the laden; the goods; the not destitute; the not bare. The expositors draw the lesson on the recompense: a recompense for their toil; the recompense; the recompense; the their toil; the unrequited; the the bondage. The witnesses observe the justice and mercy: justice and mercy in it; the justice and mercy; the justice; the mercy; the righted; the provided. The expositors note the hearts of enemies: God turns the hearts of enemies; the hearts of enemies; the the hearts; the enemies; the to favour; the his own. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I will give this people favour in the sight of the Egyptians, the that when ye go ye shall not go empty, and the favour. The verse thus records the promise of favour — teaching that God would incline the Egyptians, that Israel should depart enriched, a recompense for their bondage.
22:
"But every woman shall borrow of her neighbour, and of her that sojourneth in her house, jewels of silver, and jewels of gold, and raiment: and ye shall put them upon your sons, and upon your daughters; and ye shall spoil the Egyptians." The witnesses note God directing the women to ask jewels and raiment, so spoiling the Egyptians. Gill notes the word rendered "borrow" rather means to "ask" or desire them to give or lend; by this it appears the Israelites were spread about and mixed with the Egyptians. The witnesses note God bidding every woman ask of her neighbour jewels of silver and gold and raiment, to put on their children, and so spoil the Egyptians; the manner of the enriching, the asking of goods that justly recompensed and spoiled Egypt — that God shows how Israel should be enriched at their departure: "every woman shall borrow of her neighbour... jewels of silver, and jewels of gold, and raiment... and ye shall spoil the Egyptians"; the witnesses note the true sense of "borrow," that the word rather means to "ask" or "desire," not a fraudulent borrowing with intent never to return, but an asking of gifts which the Egyptians, glad to be rid of them, freely gave; the witnesses note the things asked, "jewels of silver, and jewels of gold, and raiment," to be put "upon your sons, and upon your daughters" — that the whole family should be enriched with the goods of Egypt; the witnesses note the justice the commentators mark, "ye shall spoil the Egyptians" — that this was a righteous recompense for the long, unpaid labour of Israel, the spoiling of those who had spoiled them, and so no wrong but justice; the witnesses note God's right to dispose of all men's goods as he will, righting his oppressed people; so that Israel is enriched and Egypt justly spoiled; and ye shall spoil the Egyptians. The witnesses note every woman shall borrow of her neighbour... jewels of silver, and jewels of gold, and raiment. The witnesses note and ye shall spoil the Egyptians.

The witnesses note God directing the women to ask jewels and raiment of their neighbours, enriching the whole family and spoiling the Egyptians — not a fraudulent borrowing but an asking of gifts freely given, a righteous recompense for Israel's long unpaid labour.

So the witnesses make the verse the just enriching and spoiling of Egypt. The lesson the expositors draw is that God bade Israel ask jewels and raiment of the Egyptians, a righteous recompense for their unpaid toil, no wrong but justice. The witnesses note the shall borrow: "every woman shall borrow" — Gill notes rather "ask" or desire; the shall borrow; the rather ask; the desire; the not fraudulent; the freely given. The expositors observe the jewels and raiment: "jewels of silver, and... gold, and raiment" — the goods of Egypt; the jewels and raiment; the silver; the gold; the raiment; the goods. The witnesses press the upon your children: "put them upon your sons... daughters" — the whole family enriched; the upon your children; the the family; the sons; the daughters; the enriched. The expositors draw the lesson on the spoil the Egyptians: "ye shall spoil the Egyptians" — a righteous recompense; the spoil the Egyptians; the a recompense; the righteous; the unpaid labour; the no wrong. The witnesses observe the no wrong but justice: no wrong but justice; the no wrong but justice; the the spoilers; the spoiled. The expositors note the God's right: God's right to dispose of all goods; the God's right; the righting. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the and ye shall spoil the Egyptians, and the spoiling. The verse thus records the just enriching of Israel — teaching that God bade them ask the goods of Egypt, a righteous recompense for their unpaid toil.
